title: Databases and Persistence
summary: Connect to SQL databases using Prisma ORM with TypeScript, write type-safe queries, and handle migrations

sections.title: An ORM Is Like a Translator Between Languages
sections.content: Your TypeScript code speaks objects and classes; your database speaks tables and SQL. An ORM (Object-Relational Mapper) is like a translator that converts between these two languages. Prisma is the leading TypeScript ORM — you define your data model in a schema file, and Prisma generates a fully typed client. This means every query is checked at compile time: typos in column names, wrong relationship paths, or mismatched types are caught before you run the code. Instead of writing 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 1' and manually mapping the result to a User object, you write 'prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id: 1 } })' and get a typed 'User | null' back.

sections.title: Defining a Prisma Model and Running Typed Queries
sections.content: Shows a Prisma schema for a blog and the generated TypeScript queries for creating and reading posts

sections.title: Migrations, Connection Pooling, and Transactions
sections.content: Migrations are version-controlled snapshots of your database schema — like git for your database structure. Prisma generates migration files when you change your schema, making deployment to production safe and repeatable. Connection pooling reuses database connections instead of creating a new one for every request — like a taxi fleet that picks up many passengers instead of each person buying a car. TypeScript's async/await makes transaction handling natural: wrap operations in 'prisma.$transaction([...])' to ensure atomicity — either all operations succeed or the entire transaction rolls back. This prevents partial updates where, for example, a payment is charged but the order is not created due to a crash.

sections.title: N+1 Query Problem — Fetching Related Data Inefficiently
sections.content: The #1 advanced database mistake is the N+1 query problem: fetching a list of items and then making a separate query for each item's relations. Wrong approach: 'const users = await prisma.user.findMany(); for (const user of users) { const posts = await prisma.post.findMany({ where: { authorId: user.id } }); }' — this makes 1 query for users + N queries for posts. Correct: use Prisma's 'include' or 'select' to eagerly load relations in a single query: 'await prisma.user.findMany({ include: { posts: true } })'. For complex cases, use raw SQL or batch queries. Always check your database query log to see what queries your ORM is actually generating.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Design a Prisma schema for an e-commerce system with User, Product, Order, and OrderItem models. A User has many Orders. An Order has many OrderItems (linking to Product). Product has name, price, and stock. Write Prisma queries to: (1) find a user with all their orders including product details, (2) reduce product stock when an order is created (use a transaction), (3) find the top 5 most ordered products. Handle the case where stock is insufficient.
hints: What relation type connects OrderItem to both Order and Product — what foreign keys are needed?
How can you update product stock and create the order atomically — what Prisma API ensures both succeed or both fail?
How do you aggregate and sort data in Prisma — can you group by product ID and count?
